---
title: "Time Horizon + Optimization Evaluations"
subtitle: "International Program on AI Evaluation Lecture"
author: "Tom Cunningham, Model Evaluation and Threat Research (METR)"
draft: true
date: 2026-04-20
bibliography: ai.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    slide-number: true
    smaller: true
    incremental: false
    sc-sb-title: true        # <- auto-show current H1/H2 in header
    slide-level: 2     
filters:
 - reveal-header
---

#       Today

1. METR's "time horizon"
2. Optimization Evaluations

#       Time Horizon


##      Basic Facts

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
![time horizon](images/2026-04-20-16-24-57.png)
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
   - Doubling every 7 months, perhaps now every 4 months.
   - We're running out of ceiling!
:::

::::

##      Time Horizon Calculation

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](images/2026-04-20-16-28-45.png)
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
- Take 200 software-engineering tasks, for each estimate the human time-to-complete.
- Evaluate a model on each task (4 repetitions)
- Fit a logistic curve between `time` and `success`, estimate where the curves passes through 50%.
:::
::::


##      Time Horizon Facts

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
Why time horizon? It's grounded.
: 
    - Time horizon correlates very well with other capability metrics, but time horizon is expensive and noisy, why generate it?
    - Remember that most capability measures are highly correlated, so pretty much *any* benchmark score will be correlated with other benchmarks.
    - Advantage of time horizon is it's *unbounded*. 
    - But could take an average of other benchmarks (e.g. Epoch Capability Index, ECI).
    - Advantage of time horizon is it's *grounded*, you can predict whether an LLM can do a task for an arbitrary new task (up t a limit).
    - Relatedly, time horizon is a much more visceral expression of model capabilities, & so has spread widely for that reason.
:::
::: {.column}
Interpreting time horizon is complicated.
: 
    - The time horizon is valid over some specific set of tasks, but how is that distribution of tasks defined?
    - METR's task set is a combination of three groups (SWAA, HCAST, RE-BENCH), none have very explicit criteria on how those tasks were selected.
    - So we see a strong and regular correlation over a somewhat opaquely chosen set of tasks.
    - No clear definition of what is a "task" vs "subtask".
:::
::::

##      Time Horizon Facts

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
We are running out of tasks.
: 
    - The CIs on the time horizons of the strongest models are very wide.
    - Not many tasks longer than 8 hours. It's a lot of work creating long tasks.


We don't have a standard theory of *why* long tasks are hard.
: 
    - The observation: LLMs are less-likely to be able to do tasks which take humans a long time.
    - Toby Ord theory: long tasks are just random combinations of short tasks (constant hazard rate). Doesn't fit the data very well, & doesn't explain why LLMs are *distinctively* worse at long tasks.
:::
::: {.column}

Be careful about "reliability"
: 
    - We say "Opus 4.6 has 50% time horizon of 10 hours"
    - It's confusing to call this "reliability".
    - The 50% does *not* refer to probability of success *within* a task, it's mostly *between* tasks, i.e. most tasks have success at either 0% or 100%.
    - If a task has an intermediate success rates, you can usually drive it to 100% with more tokens.
:::
::::



#       Optimization


1. ML training benchmarks.
    - RE-bench, MLE-bench, PostTrainBench, MLRC-bench, WeirdML, AIRS-bench, MLGymBench, SLDBench, MLAgentGench, NanoGPTSpeedrun.
2. Optimization benchmarks.
    - AlgoTune, KernelBench, SWE-fficiency, GSO-bench, Anthropic's takehome, Sakana ALE-bench, TTT-Discover's problems.
3. Leaderboards
    - NanoGPT, Terence Tao's optimization problems, Open problems (FrontierMath, Erdős, OpenProblems-Bio)

Grading continuous outputs is complicated.
: 
    - Outcomes can be very skew, e.g. GSO-bench: many small outcomes, some very large.
    - Can choose which to fix, which to optimize:
        - Maximize accuracy, fixed training time.
        - Minimize training time, fixed accuracy.
        - Minimize training data, fixed time & accuracy.

Continuous outputs are .
: 

Optimization includes superhuman performance.
: 


##          Status of Optimization 

Agents are improving on the state-of-the-art in well-studied optimization problems.
: 
    - [AlphaEvolve](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.13131) (@novikov2025alphaevolve, June 2025): 23% speedup on a key Gemini training kernel and 0.7% of Google's worldwide compute recovered through a better scheduling heuristic.

    - Andrej Karpathy's [autoresearch](https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch) (March 2026) gets an agent to reducing validation loss from pretraining a GPT-2-small model given a fixed compute budget (one H100, ~5 minutes per training loop). Over ~2 days the agent tried ~700 changes and found ~20 additive edits, yielding an ~11% improvement in "Time-to-GPT-2". Andrej Karpathy [says](https://x.com/karpathy/status/2031135152349524125) *"all the adjustments are 'real', I didn't find them manually previously, and they stack up and actually improved nanochat."*

    - [nanoGPT speedrun](https://github.com/KellerJordan/modded-nanogpt) (@kellerjordan2026moddednanogpt) is a public competition to minimize training time for GPT-2 given a fixed target loss, which has brought training time from 45 min down to 1.4 min over 77 records since May 2024. Four recent improvements are tagged as contributed with the help of "AI systems".

    - [TTT-Discover](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.16175) (@yuksekgonul2026learning, January 2026), a test-time training method, optimized the TriMul GPU kernel used in AlphaFold, achieving >15% improvement over the best human implementations. The [authors](https://www.gpumode.com/leaderboard/496?tab=rankings) of the TriMul task, expert kernel engineers, called it "legit" and noted the strategy was “similar to the current best humans, but executed better,” with most human solutions falling behind on fusing more complex operators together.



Some optimizations are *deeper* than others.
: 
    The nanoGPT speedrun provides a useful case study as a public ledger of cumulative human effort on an AI R&D problem. Some *deep* contributions, from humans, are:

    - [*Muon*](https://x.com/kellerjordan0/status/1842300916864844014) (October 2024) came from original research in the nanogpt codebase on Newton-Schulz orthogonalization, cutting training time by 21% (31.4 → 24.9 min). It has since been adopted widely, including by Kimi K2 (1T MoE), GLM-4.5 (355B MoE), and Arcee Trinity (400B MoE), and is now part of PyTorch's standard optimizer suite.
    - [*U-Net skip connections*](https://x.com/kellerjordan0/status/1856053121103093922) (November 2024) applied an encoder-decoder pattern from 2015 computer vision to transformer layers, yielding an 8% speedup (7.8 → 7.2 min). This became foundational and later records kept building on it.
    - [*Paired Head Attention*](https://x.com/classiclarryd/status/2008963501688324228) (January 2026) is a novel attention mechanism that interleaves K/Q/V across head pairs to double the effective sequence length in attention.

    These required theoretical insight, cross-domain transfer, or novel architectural ideas. 
    
    Many other records in the speedrun are imported from other projects, e.g. [bundling known techniques like ReLU² and QK-norm](https://x.com/kellerjordan0/status/1845865698532450646) (32% speedup), [importing FlexAttention](https://x.com/kellerjordan0/status/1859331370268623321) (30% speedup), (5% speedup), or [upgrading PyTorch](https://x.com/kellerjordan0/status/1847358578686152764) (8% speedup).

    Others could be described as "shallow", e.g. [lowering the logit softcap from 30 to 15](https://x.com/kellerjordan0/status/1876048851158880624).

    <!-- [lowering the logit softcap from 30 to 15](https://x.com/kellerjordan0/status/1876048851158880624)  -->


Agent optimizations are often described as *shallow*.
: 
    - Several AI R&D and optimization benchmarks, such as [MLGymBench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.14499) (@nathani2025mlgym), [GSO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.13458) (@shetty2025gso), and [SWE-fficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.06090) (@ma2025swefficiency), report that agents achieve "surface-level speedups" but "fail to discover algorithmic innovations." For instance, one of the largest speedups in [AlgoTune](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.15887) (@press2025algotune) is a 142× on a graph communicability task, achieved by replacing pure Python with BLAS calls.

    - In nanoGPT speedrun the AI-contributed patches appear to be shallow relative to the optimizations above (e.g., Muon's 21% speedup): replacing Python loops with faster library calls ([hiverge.ai](https://github.com/KellerJordan/modded-nanogpt/pull/125), ~1.2%) and combining two GPU operations to avoid writing intermediate results to memory ([Locus](https://x.com/classiclarryd/status/2012927211448516796), ~0.9%). These can be classified as typical optimization techniques that apply to many problems.

    - In autoresearch Karpathy says *"It's not novel, ground-breaking 'research' (yet), but all the adjustments are 'real'"*. The improvements that worked were things like adjusting AdamW constants, adding a scalar multiplier for QKnorm, and even making [random seed changes](https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch/issues/131). Overall he notes that the agents feel "cagey" on open-ended ideas.

    - Terence Tao has described the contribution of AI to mathematical discovery:

        > "What AI has been very good at is systematically exploring this long tail and knocking off the easiest of the problems." ([ref](https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/2026/02/ai-math-terrance-tao/686107/)):

        > "Fifty-odd problems have been solved with AI assistance, which is great, but there’s like six hundred to go. People are still chipping away at one or two of these right now." ([ref](https://www.dwarkesh.com/p/terence-tao))



##          Apple-Picking


# Example benchmarks



# Links

- Manish — [Benchmarks for evaluation](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1EAF5jPL5JUJiDioD_2NrCBceQaqsbTb4QGCsb6FAddo/edit?gid=0#gid=0)
- <https://ai-evaluation.org/>
- [Syllabus][syllabus]
- [Faculty Brief](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1KFrGIMTGhQolYh0G1EL96ch9eovmO0TRpxmSxz5qwnc/edit?tab=t.0)

[syllabus]: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1dtpP5azIsKD3Q0I7izqyjYi98vCtfRi7OoNbbicoyr8/edit?tab=t.esvbkslx9eko#heading=h.ee9uj04to8l4
